parts.heading: An Architect's Choice
parts.body:
Did you know the Royal Observatory, Greenwich was originally built on the foundations of a much older structure, Duke Humphrey's Tower, the forerunner of Greenwich Castle? This decision meant the observatory's alignment was 13 degrees off true North, a detail that reportedly displeased John Flamsteed, the first Astronomer Royal.

The observatory was commissioned by King Charles II on June 22, 1675, with the foundation stone laid on August 10 of that year. The King sought a solution for the pressing issue of determining longitude at sea, a challenge critical for the "perfecting of the art of navigation."

Sir Christopher Wren, a former Savilian Professor of Astronomy, chose the hilltop site of Greenwich Castle. This location, already a royal estate, meant no new land needed to be purchased, streamlining the project. The building, often called "Flamsteed House" in honor of its inaugural occupant, was finished by the summer of 1676.
parts.pullQuote: This decision meant the observatory's alignment was 13 degrees off true North, a detail that reportedly displeased John Flamsteed, the first Astronomer Royal.

parts.heading: The King's Charge
parts.body:
King Charles II didn't just commission a building; he also established the esteemed position of Astronomer Royal. This individual was tasked with directing the observatory and applying themselves "with the most exact care and diligence to the rectifying of the tables of the motions of the heavens, and the places of the fixed stars, so as to find out the so much desired longitude of places for the perfecting of the art of navigation."

John Flamsteed was the first to answer this call. Sir Jonas Moore, the Surveyor-General of the Ordnance, had been instrumental in persuading the King to create the observatory and installed Flamsteed as its director. The Ordnance Office was responsible for construction, with Moore personally providing essential instruments.

Flamsteed House, designed by Wren with likely assistance from Robert Hooke, became Britain's first purpose-built scientific research facility. It was constructed for £520, slightly over budget, using largely recycled materials, a testament to efficiency in its era.
parts.pullQuote: King Charles II didn't just commission a building; he also established the esteemed position of Astronomer Royal.

parts.heading: Precise Timekeeping's Dawn
parts.body:
Within the observatory's 20-foot-high Octagon Room, two remarkable clocks, built by Thomas Tompion, were installed. These unique timepieces featured 13-foot-long pendulums mounted above their faces, delivering an unparalleled accuracy of seven seconds per day, with a period of four seconds.

Flamsteed House served as the residence for the Astronomer Royal, his assistant, and family, alongside housing the critical scientific instruments. The institution grew, fostered by its connections to government boards like the Board of Ordnance and Board of Longitude, and oversight by a Board of Visitors established in 1710.

By the late 18th century, the observatory's responsibilities expanded significantly. It began publishing The Nautical Almanac, advised the government on technical matters, disseminated time, conducted meteorological and magnetic observations, and even ventured into astrophotography and spectroscopy. This expansion naturally led to an increase in both physical space and staff.
parts.pullQuote: Within the observatory's 20-foot-high Octagon Room, two remarkable clocks, built by Thomas Tompion, were installed.

parts.heading: Charting the Cosmos
parts.body:
When the observatory opened its doors in 1675, Tycho Brahe's 1000-star catalogue from 1598 was among the best available, yet it wasn't precise enough for accurate longitude determination. Flamsteed's initial task was to rectify this, creating far more accurate star charts.

Later, James Bradley, the third Astronomer Royal, significantly advanced this work. Between 1750 and 1762, Bradley charted an astonishing sixty thousand stars with such precision that his catalogues remained in use until the 1940s.

In 1835, George Biddell Airy, the new Astronomer Royal, initiated a plan to upgrade the observatory's instrumentation. The transit circle he installed in 1851 became a cornerstone for positional astronomy, used for a century to track the precise locations of celestial bodies.
parts.pullQuote: Between 1750 and 1762, Bradley charted an astonishing sixty thousand stars with such precision that his catalogues remained in use until the 1940s.

parts.heading: The World's Meridian
parts.body:
British astronomers had long used the Royal Observatory as a fundamental basis for measurement. Four distinct meridians have, in fact, passed through its buildings, defined by successive instruments.

At the International Meridian Conference in Washington, D.C., on October 22, 1884, a pivotal decision was made: the meridian passing through Airy's transit circle, first used in 1851, was adopted as the world's Prime Meridian. This established Greenwich as the global standard for mapping and timekeeping, a role it held for a century.

Today, a brass, later stainless steel, strip in the Observatory's courtyard marks this historical line. Since December 16, 1999, a powerful green laser beam shines north across the London night sky, visually representing the iconic Prime Meridian. However, the IERS Reference Meridian, approximately 102 meters east, has since replaced it as the modern geodetic standard.
parts.pullQuote: At the International Meridian Conference in Washington, D.C., on October 22, 1884, a pivotal decision was made: the meridian passing through Airy's transit circle, first used in 1851, was adopted as the world's Prime Meridian.

parts.heading: Greenwich Mean Time
parts.body:
The Airy Transit Circle, in operation from 1851 to 1938, was a crucial instrument for determining time. It was universally agreed that the "meridian line marked by the cross-hairs in the Airy Transit Circle eyepiece would indicate 0° longitude and the start of the Universal Day."

Time was determined by precisely noting when a "clock star" – a star with a known, accurate location – passed through the telescope's aimpoint. This same instrument could also be used in reverse to create those accurate star charts.

By 1925, confusion arose regarding whether Greenwich Mean Time (GMT) was reckoned from noon or midnight. This led the International Astronomical Union (IAU) in 1928 to retire GMT for astronomical and chronological purposes, replacing it with Universal Time (UT). In 1929, UT was further refined as a statistical combination of measurements from multiple observatories. While the term "GMT" persists, modern civil time is based on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), an amalgam of UT1 and International Atomic Time (TAI).
parts.pullQuote: It was universally agreed that the "meridian line marked by the cross-hairs in the Airy Transit Circle eyepiece would indicate 0° longitude and the start of the Universal Day."

parts.heading: The Iconic Time Ball
parts.body:
In 1833, Astronomer Royal John Pond introduced a highly visible public time signal: the red time ball of Greenwich. Designed to help mariners and others within sight of the observatory synchronize their clocks, it drops precisely at 1 pm (13:00) every day atop the observatory.

The impressive five-foot-diameter ball, originally made of wood and leather, would begin its ascent at 12:55 pm, reaching the top at 12:58 pm before its dramatic drop at 1 pm. Initially, an operator would manually trigger the drop, but from 1852, it became automated via an electric impulse from the Shepherd Master Clock.

The time ball was incredibly popular, serving chronometer makers, railway schedules, and the maritime community. It provided a reliable visual time signal, so much so that there was a petition for another time ball to be established in Southampton.
parts.pullQuote: In 1833, Astronomer Royal John Pond introduced a highly visible public time signal: the red time ball of Greenwich.

parts.heading: Turbulence and Transitions
parts.body:
The 1890s saw significant developments at the observatory, including the installation of the 28-inch Grubb refractor in the Great Equatorial Dome. This necessitated the construction of the famous "onion dome," expanding beyond the turret's diameter to accommodate the larger telescope.

However, this era also brought an unsettling event. On February 15, 1894, the observatory was the target of an attempted bombing, possibly Britain's first "international terrorist" incident. A 26-year-old French anarchist, Martial Bourdin, accidentally detonated a bomb in Greenwich Park, near the observatory, dying shortly after. The reason for targeting the observatory, or if it was the intended detonation site, remains unknown; the incident later inspired Joseph Conrad's 1907 novel, *The Secret Agent*.

In the early 20th century, the observatory began a gradual relocation. By 1924, electrification of nearby railways impacted magnetic and meteorological readings, prompting the move of these departments to Abinger in Surrey. After the onset of World War II in 1939, many departments were temporarily evacuated from Greenwich, and in 1940, during the Blitz, the Courtyard gates were destroyed by a direct bomb hit.
parts.pullQuote: On February 15, 1894, the observatory was the target of an attempted bombing, possibly Britain's first "international terrorist" incident.

parts.heading: A New Chapter
parts.body:
Post-World War II, in 1947, the decision was made to relocate the Royal Observatory entirely due to increasing light pollution in London. The chosen new home was Herstmonceux Castle in East Sussex, 70 km south-southeast of Greenwich.

The move was completed in stages, with the Astronomer Royal, Harold Spencer Jones, moving to Herstmonceux in 1948, and the scientific staff following once the new buildings were finished in 1957. The institution was then known as the Royal Greenwich Observatory, Herstmonceux, and once again consolidated dispersed departments like the Nautical Almanac Office and the Chronometer Department.

In 1990, the Royal Greenwich Observatory moved again, this time to Cambridge, focusing on technical support for British-owned telescopes abroad. The institution ultimately closed in 1998, with the Greenwich site returning to its original name, the Royal Observatory, Greenwich, and becoming part of the National Maritime Museum. Today, the Annie Maunder Astrographic Telescope (AMAT), a cluster of four instruments, was installed in 2018 for astronomical research, marking a return to active scientific use for the historic Greenwich site.
parts.pullQuote: Post-World War II, in 1947, the decision was made to relocate the Royal Observatory entirely due to increasing light pollution in London.
